title: Help! My Dog Swallowed a Sock: Emergency Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog swallowed a sock, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is vital and how to assist your vet with photos.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your dog swallows a sock, it is a high-urgency emergency that can cause life-threatening intestinal blockages; contact your veterinarian or an emergency animal hospital immediately.

What is gastrointestinal foreign body ingestion and why is a swallowed sock dangerous?

When a dog swallows a non-food item like a sock, it is referred to as foreign body ingestion. Unlike food, fabric does not break down in the digestive tract. A sock can become lodged in the stomach or create a life-threatening obstruction in the small intestines, preventing the passage of food and fluids.

Is it an emergency if my dog swallows a sock?

  • Yes, the urgency level for this situation is high.
  • While some small items may pass, a sock is often large enough to cause a complete blockage.
  • If left untreated, this can lead to dehydration, bowel necrosis, or even a ruptured intestine.
  • You should contact your veterinarian or the nearest emergency animal hospital immediately.

How can a photo of the swallowed item help the veterinary triage team?

  • If you have the matching pair to the swallowed sock, or a similar item, please take a clear photo of it to share with the veterinary team.
  • A photo allows the doctor to assess the size, texture, and thickness of the material.
  • This information helps them decide the best course of action, such as inducing vomiting, performing an endoscopy, or preparing for emergency surgery.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of suspected foreign body ingestion, such as a sock, triage involves assessing the patient's overall condition and prioritizing treatment based on the severity of clinical signs. Profuse vomiting or diarrhea warrants immediate attention. Initial evaluation includes assessing mucous membrane color (pink, pale/white, cyanotic, or yellow), capillary refill time (normal: 1-2 seconds), heart rate (normal: 60-120 bpm in large dogs, 70-120 bpm in small dogs, 120-200 bpm in cats; tachycardia: >180 bpm in dogs, >220 bpm in cats), pulse quality (strong/synchronous, irregular, bounding, weak/absent), and level of consciousness (alert, depressed/obtunded). Radiographs are indicated to determine the location and size of the foreign body.

Chapter: Emergency, Toxicology, Surgery, Cardiology, Pulmonology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1660)

faqs.question: Can a dog pass a sock on their own?
faqs.answer: While it is physically possible for some large dogs to pass small socks, the risk of a life-threatening obstruction is extremely high. You should never 'wait and see' without professional medical advice.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a veterinarian. Socks can become stuck in the esophagus or cause choking when coming back up, making the situation much worse.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a blockage?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include repeated vomiting, loss of appetite, lethargy, abdominal pain, and straining to have a bowel movement.
